title: Heads roll at 'big four' firm after whistleblower report
text:
The chief executive of KPMG Australia has resigned after an investigation into the accounting giant's handling of a whistleblower's allegations.

KPMG chair Martin Sheppard has accepted the resignation of chief executive Andrew Yates effective immediately, the firm announced in a release today.

KPMG's national managing partner audit and assurange Julian McPherson will also stand down from his role effective immediately and will resign after transitioning his client responsibilities.

KPMG chief executive Andrew Yates has resigned. (Supplied)

The shake-up comes after ﻿legal firm Allens was appointed by the company to investigate its prior internal and external investigations, and found that they had fallen short of the rigour required to investigate a whistleblower's claim that client documents were being inappropriately shared internally.

"I have been committed to a speak-up culture in our firm, it is clear that in this case we have let ourselves down and I take accountability," Yates said.

﻿Stan Stavros has been appointed interim chief executive. ﻿

KPMG is one of the "big four" global accounting firms, alongside Ernst&Young, Deloitte, and PwC.﻿

"We apologise unreservedly to the whistleblower. We commit to learning from this process to ensure we create an environment where it is safe and easy to surface concerns that will be acted upon," Sheppard said.

"KPMG apologises to the clients whose information was not handled with the care and respect they expect from us. We also apologise to our people – as these matters do not reflect on the contribution they make to KPMG and our clients."

KPMG is one of the "big four" professional services firms. (Getty)

Sheppard said Allens would continue to investigate the whistleblower's allegations, while KPMG has also engaged leading ethical organisational culture specialist Prin﻿cipia Advisory for a separate review into the firm's policies and processes to support people to speak up.

"KPMG is committed to transparency and will publish the findings of the Principia review. We will move swiftly to act upon their recommendations," Sheppard said.

"We are reinforcing and strengthening the controls that protect client confidentiality, and we will set out for our clients the specific steps we are taking to keep their information protected. For each of our audit clients we will confirm that any conduct matters do not impact the quality of their audits."

Sheppard said he knew KPMG would have to work on rebuilding trust.

"That's why we are not asking anyone to take our word for it, and we are inviting scrutiny and challenge on our remedial actions," he said.

The whistleblower's initial allegations about the inappropriate internal sharing of client information were not substantiated by an initial internal investigation or an external legal review of that investigation.

However, KPMG acknowledged that the initial investigation "was not conducted with the necessary rigour".

title: Why millions of middle-aged Aussie men are driven to drink
text:
More than two million middle-aged men in Australia are drinking alcohol at risky levels, a first-of-its-kind study has found.

The national in-depth study from Flinders University analysed national data from the 2019 and 2022-23 National Drug Strategy Household Survey to see what was driving Aussie men to drink.

Almost half (43 per cent) of men aged 30 to 59 exceeded Australia's national alcohol guidelines, which recommend no more than 10 standard drinks a week, and no more than four on any one day.

Middle-aged Australian men are drinking at risky levels. (Getty)

Lead author and Flinders University PhD candidate, Stefano Zaccagnini, says the study found risky drinking was more common among men who smoke, use illicit drugs, experience high psychological distress, live in regional areas, or earn higher household incomes.

Men working in managerial, trade and technical occupations were also more likely to drink above recommended limits.

"Our findings show that more than 2.2 million Australian men aged 30 to 59 are drinking in excess of the national alcohol guidelines," Zaccagnini said.

Heavy drinking is motivated by multiple factors, a new study has found. (Getty)

"Men drink alcohol for many reasons and across different social and cultural settings, and at consistently higher rates than women, which makes this an important group to study.

"Midlife is also a period when drinking can shift from occasional to routine – for example as a regular way to unwind – and that can heighten the risk of both short-term and long-term alcohol-related harms such as accidents, injuries, assaults, and chronic illnesses including dependence, liver disease and cancer."

The findings also highlight important differences within the midlife category.

Men aged 30 to 44 who reported high psychological distress, were significantly more likely to drink at risky levels compared to those with lower psychological distress.

In contrast, psychological distress was not strongly linked to risky drinking among men aged 45 to 59.

"This research suggests that stress and mental health may influence drinking differently at various stages of midlife," Zaccagnini said.

Senior researcher and Director of Flinders' National Centre for Education and Training on Addiction (NCETA), Professor Jacqueline Bowden, said the findings challenge common assumptions about who is most at risk of alcohol-related harms.

"There's a perception that risky drinking is mainly a problem for younger people, but this research shows it remains very common among men in midlife," she said.

"Whilst risky drinking among Australian men has declined overall in recent decades, progress has been slower among men in midlife, a group often missed by prevention efforts that tend to focus more on younger people. For many middle-aged men, alcohol is closely tied to work culture, social life and managing stress during these years."

Alcohol and Drug Foundation chief executive Dr Erin Lalor AM said this research reiterated the need for stronger investments in tailored approaches aimed at reducing alcohol-related harms.

"We know that a 'one size fits all' approach doesn't work when it comes to addressing the complex drivers of risky drinking. We need stronger investments by governments to help roll out targeted, evidence-based campaigns and programs," she said.

title: Musicians back out of Trump's Washington concert after widespread criticism
text:
A series of musicians announced for a concert organised by the Trump administration have pulled out a day after the line-up was revealed.

The Freedom 250 show at Washington's National Mall was set to host a long list of bands and singers as part of the Great American State Fair.

But already several have pulled out of the event.

Young MC performing at a 'I Love the '90s' show. (AP)

Young MC, best known for his 1989 hit Bust A Move , decried the concert.

"The artists were never told about any political involvement with the event," he said.

"I hope to perform in DC in the near future at an event that is not so politically charged."

Motown group The Commodores have also backed out.

"Our music has always been our voice and we choose not to publicly affiliate with any single political party. We support the betterment of all Americans," the group said.

Funk singer Morris Day denied he was ever booked for the gig.

"Contrary to rumour, Morris Day and The Time will not be performing at the 'Great American State Fair'," he said on Instagram.

"It's a no from me."

Country singer Martina McBride also pulled out.﻿

There is also confusion about the inclusion of Milli Vanilli on the line-up. The Grammy-winning duo were revealed as a musical hoax in 1990, lip-syncing songs performed by other people.

European R&B duo Milli Vanilli were stripped of their Grammy in 1990. (Nine Archives)

The actual singers have distanced themselves from the concert, claiming they were not invited. The only remaining member of the hoax duo may still perform, but it is not known if he will be singing.

But rapper Freedom Williams of C+C Music Factory gave a profane and bizarre defence of his booking.

In a seven-minute video delivered while sitting on the toilet, Williams explained his position.

Musician Freedom Williams issued a statement about the America 250 show from a toilet. (Instagram)

"I will vote for fucking f—ing Genghis Khan, Hitler, and motherf—ing Ivan the Terrible before I let you n—s tell me what to motherf—king do," he said.

"You know who 90 per cent of the people was at our shows? White people. You know who 70 per cent of those white people probably voted for? Republicans. So you n—s don't fucking count."

His diatribe took aim at Barack Obama , Bill Clinton and the COVID vaccine.

He also spoke in favour of late Libyan dictator Muammar Gaddafi , Donald Trump and Vanilla Ice.

Vanilla Ice is the biggest name remaining on the lineup. (Getty)

Vanilla Ice has confirmed he will be performing at the gig.

Also on the line-up are Flo Rida and Bret Michaels.

title: Man seemingly caught asleep while driving on major motorway
text:
A Tesla driver has been caught seemingly asleep behind the wheel while driving on one of Sydney's busiest motorways.

The video, shared on Instagram, appears to show the man with his eyes closed and leaning back in his seat. His hands are not on the wheel of his white Tesla.

A geolocation tag on the post suggests the man is driving on the M2 Motorway, a major roadway that travels from Sydney's north-west towards the North Shore and the Sydney Harbour Bridge.

The driver acknowledged the person videoing him. (Instagram/@luke.bandiera)

The post was captioned with "arvo snooze", in a reference to the driver appearing to be asleep despite being in the driver's seat of the car.

After a few seconds, the man, who is wearing a seatbelt, opens his eyes and puts his hands on the steering wheel, waving a hand towards the person filming him, potentially looking to signal he is alert and awake.

The man was most likely using Tesla's Autopilot mode, which can allow the driver to use cruise control to maintain the speed of the car while also holding a safe distance between other cars on the road.

Technically speaking, drivers using Tesla's autopilot mode are supposed to be "fully attentive". (Instagram/@luke.bandiera)

It also offers an autosteer setting, where the car maintains a set speed if there is no car in front, and automatically detects lane markings, road edges and the presence of other objects on the road to keep the car in its lane.

Tesla's official website states users must still be fully attentive while engaged in Autopilot mode.

It says that if the car detects the driver not paying enough attention, they will be locked out of autopilot mode.

title: Coroner finds baby's death after home birth was preventable
text:
A coroner has found the death of a baby after a home birth went wrong in central Victoria could have been avoided.

'Baby R' died from perinatal hypoxia in August 2022, six days after his mother rushed to Bendigo Hospital for an emergency caesarean.

Coroner Dimitra Dubrow delivered her findings today that Baby R's mother was not suitable for a home birth, and had not been adequately advised to be able to make a fully informed decision.

The inquest heard Baby R's mother wanted to give birth at home after a "traumatic" experience with her first child, who was delivered by emergency c-section at Bendigo hospital after an obstructed labour.

"Baby R's mother was outside the recommended care for home birth by reason of her previous caesarean section and her pregnancy not being low-risk," Ms Dubrow said.

She also found Baby R's mother's private midwife, Elizabeth Murphy, did not act in accordance with Australian College of Midwifery (ACM) guidelines, and the care provided alongside another midwife during labour was "deficient".

Elizabeth Murphy was Baby R's mother's primary midwife for the home birth. (Supplied: Internet Archive)

A lawyer read a statement on behalf of Baby R's family outside the Coroners Court of Victoria after the findings were delivered.

The family cannot be identified for legal reasons.

"At the start of the coronial process, we were told it was happening for one simple reason: our baby's life mattered," Isabelle McCombe from Slater and Gordon lawyers said in a statement.

"That truth has been an anchor for our family as we've waded through the guilt, fear, anger, silence, shame and pain we've carried for the past three and a half years.

" A loss we will carry for the rest of our lives. "

The family said it may provide further comment after time to reflect on the findings.

'What if' moment

At a week-long coronial hearing last year, Baby R's mother described a "what if" moment during the home birth, when she noticed meconium liquor about 3:10pm.

Meconium liquor is waste usually passed by a baby after birth.

She had been in labour since the early hours of the morning.

In her statement to the court, the mother recalled saying "oh f***", and assumed she would be on her way to hospital.

"I remember [the midwife] Marie-Louise just saying, 'We'll monitor you more closely'," the mother said in her statement.

"I wish I'd asked what 'more monitoring' looked like, to discern if we were on the same page. I didn't do this, and this is hard to live with."

The baby's mother was herself a registered midwife and had worked as both a nurse and midwife before leaving healthcare during her first pregnancy.

Ms Dubrow found the presence of meconium liquor should have prompted discussion about transfer to hospital and the midwives to consult Bendigo Hospital.

"If Baby R's birth occurred in hospital, not at home, or transfer to hospital occurred at or around 3:30pm, I consider Baby R's death would've been avoided," she said.

The court heard there were signs of an obstructed labour and at 2:45pm Baby R's mother said she "felt stuck like last time".

At 7:43pm a fetal heart rate of 195 beats per minute (bpm) was recorded and Ms Murphy advised there were signs of distress and a transfer was required.

Midwife regrets

Two private midwives present during the home birth of Baby R, Marie-Louise Lapeyre and Elizabeth Murphy conceded their care was inadequate when complications arose, and that a transfer to the hospital should have happened sooner.

Midwife Marie-Louise Lapeyre outside the Victorian Coroners Court in July. (ABC News)

Following investigations by the Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency and the Nursing and Midwifery Board, both midwives were told they cannot practise as private midwives, along with other conditions.

Ms Dubrow found Ms Murphy did not act in accordance with ACM guidelines by stating Baby R's mother did not require a specialist obstetrician consultation at 36 weeks, and providing Bendigo Hospital with incomplete information about her obstetric history.

"As her primary care provider Ms Murphy had an obligation to inform Baby R's mother home birth was against recommended care," Ms Dubrow said.

"Instead Ms Murphy reassured Baby R's mother … that she was suitable [for home birth]."

Ms Dubrow also found Castlemaine GP Veronica Moule should have communicated better with Ms Murphy about the risks associated with Baby R's mother's pregnancy in her referral.

Ms Dubrow recommended the Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists and ACM review the finding, and consider how guidance documents could be streamlined for those providing maternity care and families seeking information.

She also recommended ACM consider clarifying its guidelines for consultation and referrals.

title: Major winemaker threatens to move business overseas if tax changes go through
text:
A major South Australian winemaker is threatening to move the business internationally in response to proposed tax changes.

The federal budget included a 30 per cent minimum tax on both discretionary trusts and capital gains.

Third-generation winemaker Mitchell Taylor, from Taylors Wines in the Clare Valley, said if the changes came into effect, the business would "seriously have to consider" moving out of Australia.

Managing director of Taylors Wines, Mitchell Taylor. (Supplied: Taylors Wines)

"We're already the highest taxed wine-producing country in the world," Mr Taylor said.

Future of farm trusts Despite winning a carve-out to changes to family trusts announced in the budget, farmers are still worried about what tax reform means for them.

"We've got the 29 per cent [Wine Equalisation Tax] WET and then the GST on top of that. So we've effectively got a 42 per cent wholesale tax where no other industry has."

Mr Taylor said the business used trusts for asset protection and family support.

"The reason we have these trusts is so that we can succeed, so that my grandfather could pass it on to my father, then I can pass on to the next generation," he said.

"I lost my dear sister at a young age and she had a very young family. Her dying wish was the business would look after her kids.

"So, [we] set up a trust to look after another generation of the Taylor family."

With no end in sight to an industry crisis led by extreme weather, an oversupply, and a shift in consumer preference, Mr Taylor expected other businesses would "look at their options" too.

"If we let all these tax changes go through, it will devastate our ability to be competitive, our ability to grow and our ability to invest and employ people in the great regions throughout Australia," he said.

Calls for change

South Australia's opposition minister for small and family business Ben Hood said the state government could increase the payroll tax threshold to help struggling businesses like those in the wine industry.

"We would like to see the government looking to increase the threshold on payroll tax from $1.5 million to $2.1 million because it will be able to assist a lot of businesses here in South Australia," Mr Hood said.

" Especially those bumping their head up against that arbitrary ceiling and they're not being able to employ more people. "

Small and family business minister Nadia Clancy said South Australia already had the second-highest payroll tax threshold.

She said she had been working with Primary Industries Minister Clare Scriven to help the wine industry.

"We will continue to do what we can on our level of government just to support small and family businesses and to make sure we have the best settings," she said, without directly commenting on changes to capital gains tax or the tax on trust incomes.

Despite the threat from Mr Taylor to move, Treasurer Tom Koutsantonis said South Australia remained "the best state in Australia to do business".

Tom Koutsantonis says South Australia is supportive of businesses. (ABC News: Ashlin Blieschke)

"If there are federal changes that are impacting businesses and they need to restructure to try and help themselves under that, if they do it here, they won't pay taxes," he said.

"If you move overseas or interstate you will pay taxes to restructure, here you don't.

"We're the only jurisdiction in Australia to have abolished commercial transactional taxes like stamp duty."

But Mr Taylor said there needed to be more conversations between industry and government.

"There's so much uncertainty, it's pretty hard to jump at all the different proposals. You can wake up having nightmares about it," he said.

title: The economic gap between generations is on track to reach record levels
text:
When it comes to saving money, Mady, who has four children, has tried almost everything.

Her family of six, who live in Central Queensland's Rockhampton, has already cut back on subscriptions, halted renovations, and compared health cover and insurance options.

"We're finding it harder and harder and just stretched very thin to afford, well, basically groceries and everything like that," said Mady, who asked the ABC only use her first name.

"But also just to be able to save money and to continue our renovations.

"It feels like a pressure cooker because with that interest rate rise our mortgage goes up, but then you also notice everything else goes up.

"The cost of everything is rising at the same time, and it sort of feels like, 'When is it going to stop?'"

Mady says she is cutting back on school sport for her kids and looking for cheaper health care and insurance. (Supplied: Mady)

Mady's family was in the middle of renovating their home when the Reserve Bank started hiking interest rates again. Now, the work has been put on pause.

"We just had the roof redone. It was leaking until that point and it needs re-stumping," she said.

"We've got termite control needing to go in as we've got lots of termite activity outside our home and … there is a concrete cancer in some of the stumps under the house."

She is aiming to organise a garage sale with her neighbours in June to make some extra cash.

"It's definitely something we find we have to do in order to bump our savings back up," she said.

But not all Australian households are under such financial pressure.

According to research from the Actuaries Institute, inequality between the younger and older generations is widening and could reach record levels within years.

Inequality between generations is growing

While all generations are better off than they were in 2000, some of the major domains analysed by the Actuaries Institute have highlighted growing intergenerational inequity.

"There are three domains, in particular the economic and the housing and environmental, where outcomes for younger people seem to be getting worse," said Hugh Miller, one of the lead authors of the report.

"That was sort of in contrast to some of the older generations, and so we flagged that as an equity issue."

The analysis draws on 25 indicators across six domains: economic and fiscal, housing, health and disability, social, education and environment.

It tracks changes in wealth and wellbeing across Australians aged 25–34, 45–54 and 65–74 since 2000.

Hugh Miller says the outcomes for younger people are getting worse in the areas of economics, housing and environment. (Supplied: Actuaries Institute)

While Dr Miller noted many older Australians were feeling the pressures just as much as younger Australians, the broad picture for home ownership was very different.

"You won't necessarily see the same rapid gains for younger people as they accumulate wealth or because they're locked out of the housing market," Dr Miller said.

"They have less opportunity to then get in and start accumulating that wealth in that way."

Australia's dwelling value-to-income ratio has climbed to 8.2, well above the 20-year average of 6.8, according to data from Cotality.

It now takes an average of 11 years to save for a 20 per cent deposit, with an average of 45 per cent of weekly income to service a mortgage.

Saul Eslake says housing is driving intergenerational inequality. (ABC News: Maren Preuss)

Independent economist Saul Eslake said home ownership was the biggest driver of inequality among Australians.

"Home ownership among people aged under 35 is back to where it was in 1947," he said.

"Among people aged between 35 and 44, it's back to where it was in 1954.

"Whereas home ownership among people aged 65 and over hasn't changed from its peak at the census of 1966 and, of course, given how much house prices have risen, that's been of enormous advantage to older people."

Wages stagnant for years

One of the other major indicators for this widening intergenerational gap, according to Dr Miller, is wage growth, or the lack thereof.

He said for younger Australians, wages had been "very flat" for a number of years, despite inflation.

"It's sort of barely moved for over a decade and so compared to a period of Australian history where everyone's wages have been clicking up by 1 per cent above CPI over time, we just actually haven't seen that consistently," he said.

A study from the Pew Research Centre found 79 per cent of Australians think children will be financially worse off than their parents.

Australia's results were some of the worst in the world.

"The issue is whether young people will continue to have access to the same sort of ladder of wealth accumulation that others have had," said Dr Miller.

"I think people would sort of say we've had quite outsized gains in wealth and the majority of Australian wealth is in sort of equities and capital gains and both those things have outpaced incomes by quite a margin for quite a few years."

It is becoming increasingly difficult for younger Australians to enter the housing market. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

A lack of wage growth impacts things like home ownership rates, which Dr Miller said had fallen 10 percentage points over 25 years, which then impacts retirement.

"Interestingly, over the last five years, it's actually sort of plateaued or even gone up a click … and it looks like some combination of sort of the low interest rates post-COVID and some of the government incentives … might have contributed to actually a bit of that stabilisation," he said.

The federal government said housing inequality was one of the reasons it was introducing legislation to change the capital gains tax discount and negative gearing.

Environmental changes impact housing too

As seen above, Dr Miller said there was also crossover between sectors, as negative impacts from one sector impact the results of another.

"It's clear that the climate-related indicators that we track with the CO2 and temperatures are very adverse and that's a huge intergenerational issue," he said.

The impacts of climate change on the environment have the potential to further exacerbate issues with housing, according to Dr Miller.

"As extreme weather events increase and that will happen off the back of climate change, there will be increased costs, and those costs either get met through insurance and premiums that become increasingly unaffordable or through adaptation costs," he said.

Taskforce to tackle insurance unaffordability Industry experts warn we need to make homes more resilient in order to tackle unaffordable insurance premiums.

Home insurance premiums have increased by 51 per cent in the past five years, according to data analytics firm Finity.

Dr Miller emphasised that certain communities across the country were more vulnerable to a number of these issues, particularly when they could no longer insure their home.

"A lot of those communities tend to be ones that might be exposed in particular ways, and may not have economic resources to move and afford premiums," he said.

"And so there are some genuine issues that the industry and the profession have had to grapple with."

Older women negatively impacted

Mr Eslake said despite most older Australians being better off than their younger counterparts, he had seen a growing cohort of older women who were living in or close to poverty.

"That's because they have either never been able to achieve home ownership or they've fallen out of home ownership as a result [of] typically a family breakdown," he said.

"And of course, women have less super than men because they usually take time out of working to care for children or sometimes for older relatives."

According to superannuation peak body AFSA, the median superannuation balance in June 2022 for those aged 60 to 64 was $205,385 for males and $153,685 for females, a 25.2 per cent difference.

An emerging sector of concern for older Australians, according to Mr Eslake, was access to nursing homes.

"Going into aged care and finding something that's suitable and that they can afford," he said.

"And of course, this issue didn't arise so much in previous generations because most of us didn't live that long."

title: Newly discovered ancient wasp species named after F1 driver Piastri
text:
With nine grand prix victories to his name, Formula 1 drivers are well aware of the sting McLaren ace Oscar Piastri possesses.

But now it seems he has impressed more than just his F1 rivals and fans, with the motor racing world abuzz for a different reason.

Three biologists, obviously followers of Piastri, have named a newly discovered species of wasp dating from ‌the time of the dinosaurs and found ‌preserved in Burmese amber after the Australian.

A research article in the June issue of academic journal Palaeoworld named the ‌insect, found ‌in ⁠northern Myanmar and dating from ​the middle Cretaceous period, as Gwesped piastrii.

"The specific epithet honours Mr Oscar Piastri for his achievements in Formula One, and because ⁠the colour ‌of the ​amber piece recalled to the first ​author the iconic ‌McLaren orange," the article explained.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Piastri is currently attending the Isle of Man TT races with a weekend off before the Monaco ​Grand Prix on June 7.

The Cretaceous ​period ended 65 million years ago.

The 25-year-old from Melbourne finished third in last season's championship, losing out to teammate Lando Norris.

He finished just 13 points behind Norris, with four-time champion Max Verstappen second.

AAP/Reuters

title: KPMG boss quits amid auditing scandal
text:
The chief executive of accounting firm KPMG, Andrew Yates, has resigned effective immediately after the mishandling of whistleblower allegations.

"I have been committed to a 'speak-up culture' in our firm; it is clear that in this case we have let ourselves down and I take accountability," Mr Yates said in a short statement.

Auditing partner Julian McPherson has also stepped down.

"KPMG Australia confirms its treatment of a whistleblower and investigation into their allegations fell short of the firm's expectations, those of the whistleblower and the broader community," KPMG said in a statement.

It reveals a whistleblower brought allegations regarding client documents being inappropriately shared internally to the senior leadership team.

An internal investigation did not substantiate the claims, with a further external legal firm also supporting the outcome.

However, after the whistleblower raised further complaints with the board, a different external law firm, Allens, was appointed to complete another investigation into the claims, which is ongoing.

KPMG says the initial investigations were not conducted with the "necessary rigour required" and "fell short of the firm's expectations".

KPMG chair says they 'apologise unreservedly'

KPMG says the ongoing investigation by Allens has revealed a further, separate incident in which internal documents containing client information were inappropriately shared internally.

KPMG has reported this new finding to impacted clients, regulators, professional bodies and the parliamentary committee.

"We apologise unreservedly to the whistleblower," KPMG chairman Martin Sheppard said in a statement.

"We commit to learning from this process to ensure we create an environment where it is safe and easy to surface concerns that will be acted upon.

"KPMG apologises to the clients whose information was not handled with the care and respect they expect from us. We also apologise to our people, as these matters do not reflect on the contribution they make to KPMG and our clients."

Mr Sheppard said KPMG would also engage an ethics consultant to review its "speak-up culture".

"We are reinforcing and strengthening the controls that protect client confidentiality, and we will set out for our clients the specific steps we are taking to keep their information protected," he said.

"For each of our audit clients, we will confirm that any conduct matters do not impact the quality of their audits."

Stan Stavros has been appointed as interim chief executive while a process goes on to find a permanent replacement.

ASIC tells parliamentary inquiry it is investigating

Financial industry regulator ASIC has told a parliamentary committee into financial services that it is investigating a number of registered company auditors who handled a whistleblower complaint at KPMG.

"We commenced those inquiries after a meeting with KPMG on the 14th of April and then after receiving further anonymised case information in writing from KPMG," ASIC deputy Sarah Court said.

"We've identified three of the four people involved are registered company auditors … and therefore come under ASIC's regulatory oversight.

"We have been informed by KPMG that they intend to claim legal professional privilege over much of their investigation material."

A public letter from Lendlease was tabled that described several incidents where KPMG audit partners accessed Lendlease board papers.

"There is clearly a breach of what would normally occur between an auditor and its client here," ASIC chair Joe Longo told the committee.

title: Centralian Eisteddfod seeking funding support amid future uncertainty
text:
The Centralian Eisteddfod Council has raised the alarm about funding challenges, which could mean the event will cease after next year.

Council president Allison Huhs said a funding agreement with the Alice Springs Town Council (ASTC) will expire this year, and the committee had enough money to run the event through 2027, but after that, its future was uncertain.

"Three years ago, there were some changes to the council, and they basically entered into a three-year agreement with us and said after that, done," she said.

"We're hoping that there are negotiations that will open up with council again, because we've got yet another new council … we're hoping that they will see non-profit arts as a really good community engagement for kids."

Allison Huhs says eisteddfods give children the chance to perform. (ABC Alice Springs: Will Green)

ASTC interim chief executive Nicole Battle said the council had given a total of $37,500 to the eisteddfod over the three years, and the committee was welcome to apply for further sponsorship through grants, but funding was not guaranteed.

Ms Huhs said the Centralian Eisteddfod also received funding from the Department of Education, local businesses and through entry fees, but more funding was needed to run the event.

How much does it cost to run an eisteddfod?

According to the president of the Association of Eisteddfod Societies Australia, David Hague, across the country other eisteddfods are also grappling with funding challenges.

"I think you could safely say all eisteddfods around Australia are always very tight on funding," he said.

"They're non-profit organisations, and to present an eisteddfod is costing more and more every year."

Allison Huhs says the Centralian Eisteddfod cost about $35,000 each year. (ABC Alice Springs: Will Green)

Ms Huhs said the Centralian Eisteddfod cost about $35,000 each year, with a significant portion of costs arising from adjudicator flights, accommodation, meals, and fees from venue hire.

She said the committee kept entry fees to a minimum so children and families could afford to perform, but the income generated was only enough to cover the costs of the trophies.

Mr Hague said copyright fees and online ticketing systems were increasingly adding to the costs of running the events, and since COVID-19, some eisteddfods had stopped running.

"We have lost a few … there are some that have gone out of business," he said.

A shortage of volunteers is also putting pressure on eisteddfod committees. (ABC Alice Springs: Will Green)

Mr Hague said a shortage of volunteers was also putting pressure on eisteddfod committees, something Ms Huhs could confirm.

"All of our workforce is completely volunteers, we don't pay any of us to do this, and there are 300 man-hours that we put together through (the eisteddfod)," she said.

"And volunteers don't have time to give back and try to get funding all the time."

Why are eisteddfods important?

Despite the challenges of running the event, Ms Huhs said it was well worth it.

"I've been involved with a few organisations around town, and it's always the kids that are just enjoying themselves, which is the most important part of this," she said.

"Some kids, they practice, they practice, they practice. They have very little chance to just be able to perform. So we give them that platform."

Ms Huhs said children also developed self-confidence and the bravery to take risks and learn, which would serve them throughout the rest of their lives, even outside of a performance context.

David Hague said he would like to see the federal government recognise the value of eisteddfods across the country. (ABC Alice Springs: Will Green)

Mr Hague said he would like to see the federal government recognise the value of eisteddfods across the country.

"There's so many professional performers, singers, dancers, musicians, that have started their performing life in eisteddfods, and if the eisteddfods were to disappear, that's an opportunity lost for many young people," he said.

"There needs to be a recognition at all government levels and community of their importance."

title: Live: Friends, loyal listeners gather for James Valentine's public memorial
text:
A celebration of ABC presenter James Valentine's life is taking place at a public service at Sydney's Town Hall, with the memorial to include contributions from colleagues, family friends and music icons.

Valentine died aged 64 last month using voluntary assisted dying, two years after being diagnosed with cancer.

He had a 30-year career presenting radio at the ABC, most notably for the Afternoons show on 702 ABC Sydney where he developed his distinct approach to talkback radio.

Follow our live coverage of his memorial.

title: What the fight over London data centre plans tells us about the AI backlash
text:
To the passer-by, it looks like just another one of the countless brick buildings that hem in the narrow streets of inner London.

But look closer and one will notice the tower of the Truman Brewery complex, built on the city's famous Brick Lane in the early 19th Century and now an unlikely symbol of a growing flashpoint between Big Tech and local communities.

The building, which was once one of London's biggest breweries, is currently home to music venues and eateries.

Very soon, it could be a data centre, a key piece of digital infrastructure filled with server racks vital to powering artificial intelligence.

Tech companies promise AI will revolutionise the global economy, but critics are sounding the alarm over whether it could replace humans in the workplace, help spread misinformation and hurt the environment.

Locals had long been calling for the brewery site to be turned into public housing. (ABC News: Thomas Morgan)

But in the instance of the Truman Brewery plans, currently before the local council, opposition is growing not because of those threats, but because of fears it could impact the area's nightlife and economy.

The ABC has contacted developer, Grow Places, for comment.

But if AI is to revolutionise economies right around the world, as it is promised, advocates say communities will need to accept these proposals.

"Many countries are going to have a hard time building as many data centres as they need," says Keegan McBride from the Tony Blair Institute.

"Over the next five to 10 years, we're talking about trillions of dollars that is going to be spent on AI investments, from hardware to data centres to staff."

However for Oliver Hayes, head of policy and campaigns at British not-for-profit Global Action Plan, the Truman Brewery proposal is indicative of everything that is wrong with Big Tech's rollout of AI.

"The local community there has been saying for years, what we need is social housing, the area is crying out for social housing," he says of the Truman Brewery proposal.

"And instead it's proposed to be a data centre."

American resistance

While the loudest voices against the development in Brick Lane take issue with a lack of social housing locally, activists say protests against data centres are seemingly popping up for a myriad of reasons, from environmental concerns to fears over what their energy use will mean for power bills.

The backlash taking hold in small pockets of London and other Western cities is following a trend already in full swing in the United States, where major tech companies are spending hundreds of billions of dollars on constructing massive new data centres.

Miquel Villa runs Data Center Watch, a research project by AI firm 10am Labs which tracks the growing opposition to data centre developments across the US.

Data centres like this one in Virginia are becoming an increasing source of tension within many American communities. (Reuters: Leah Millis)

In 2025 alone, his project estimated local opposition delayed or blocked projects worth up to $US152 billion ($211 billion), and the issue is now getting traction in state politics.

"Opposition is getting faster, it is getting bigger and I would say it is getting more common," Mr Villa says.

Key to this change, he says, is that public resistance was moving from isolated backlash to broad anxiety and hostility towards new proposed developments, and there is now evidence those concerns are working their way into America's political debate ahead of upcoming mid-term elections.

" In the elections of November 2025, which impacted Virginia, New Jersey and also in Georgia, there were important races that were almost decided by the data centre question. "

In one state race in Virginia, candidate John McAuliff managed to flip a Republican district to the Democrats by tying the data centre issue with the cost of electricity.

Already some high-profile politicians like Bernie Sanders are talking about a national moratorium on the construction of new centres, which Mr Villa stresses is still a "remote possibility".

Mr Sanders, a two-time US presidential candidate, announced legislation in March that would stop new centres from being built "to ensure the safety of humanity".

"We need serious public debate and democratic oversight over this enormously consequential issue," he said in a statement.

Anti-AI protests are increasingly popping up in American cities amid increasing concerns about the technology's environmental impact. (Reuters: Manuel Orbegozo)

'A horror story'

The key drivers for US residents are concerns about infrastructure and the energy data centres demand, which in some communities has driven up power prices.

And the opposition is now showing signs of taking hold elsewhere.

Recent fuel protests on the streets of Ireland's capital Dublin were in part motivated by popular anger at soaring electricity use from such facilities at a time when energy supplies were being constrained by the Iran war.

Protests against Facebook's data centre plans are growing in Dublin, where the company has a significant corporate presence. (Reuters: Clodagh Kilcoyne)

Mr Hayes says Global Action Plan is also starting to see similar sentiments in parts of the UK.

"They're very worried. They look across the pond at the US in particular and see a horror story," he says.

"It's about people's utility bills being affected by data centres, the local water table being affected by data centres, and they're very worried that those things may be replicated here."

A question of sovereignty

In Australia, some experts predict new data centre developments could add the equivalent energy demand of Tasmania to the grid, fuelling concerns about whether that could delay the renewables transition.

In Britain, similar concerns are also popping up. Professor Samer Bagaeen, who is the head of town planning at the UK's University of the Built Environment, says at least 140 data centres are proposed for construction in the UK alone.

Each one of those centres consumes the equivalent electricity demand of a small town. If they are all built, Professor Bagaeen says that would effectively double the UK's peak energy demand.

Experts warn AI data centres could keep fossil fuels online A new report concludes the AI-fuelled surge of power-hungry data centres across Australia is jeopardising the country's energy transition.

"You're not going to get all of them being built in one go, but certainly the power demand from those will be substantial," he says.

But advocates for data centres like Mr McBride contend many of those concerns about energy and water consumption are being addressed by more efficient designs.

Instead, they say Australia and the UK need to keep pace or face losing out economically and strategically.

"Not participating in the revolution that is happening at the moment is basically a recipe for disaster," Mr McBride says.

"If you as a country aren't building and don't have your own compute capacity … you're going to fall behind and you're going to lose control over what you can and cannot do in the future."

Can AI and communities co-exist?

For Mr Hayes, the lack of transparency around what purpose data centres were serving was helping feed public mistrust in them.

"What's it actually for is never answered, right?" he says.

" If you said to a community, right 'this data centre is essential for this university hospital working out how to crack this horrific disease', well, that's very different to 'we need this data centre so that Grok can nudify teens'. "

And with growing concern about the environmental impact of data centres, Professor Bagaeen says governments could pursue "multiple possible answers" to restore public confidence.

He said that included mandating that tech companies and developers contribute towards infrastructure upgrades to clear bottlenecks in the system.

"Another one is maybe accelerated investment in renewable generation and then target data centres in those areas where you have substantial renewable electricity generation."

"It doesn't necessarily solve the water problem, but it solves the grid problem."

Governments are considering whether the approval of new data centres should depend on the delivery of more renewable energy. (ABC News: Angus Verley)

But Mr McBride says, if everyday people are going to benefit from AI, they are going to need to accept its arrival and governments are going to need to do the hard yards convincing voters of its benefits.

He lists cutting down on hospital waitlists a more efficient public sector as possible uses for AI if properly implemented.

"AI is already incredibly capable, incredibly transformative, and it's having an impact across a whole host of sectors, from national security to healthcare to government.

"I have a hard time imagining that we're going to put this genie back in the bottle. In fact, I would pretty much guarantee we won't at the current level of capability.

"And I think governments have, to be honest, done a pretty poor job at being able to sell that positive vision."

title: Firefighters look to restart PFAS case against manufacturer 3M
text:
The head of Fire Rescue Victoria (FRV) says the federal government's decision to sue a "forever chemical" manufacturer has given him hope of restarting a similar case on behalf of Australia's firefighters.

The Commonwealth is seeking $2 billion from multinational company 3M, the manufacturer of a firefighting foam containing PFAS that contaminated 28 Australian Defence Force bases.

It is the largest legal claim ever brought by the Commonwealth.

FRV was preparing to be the lead litigant in a class action against 3M in 2023 until then-state emergency services minister Jaclyn Symes intervened to stop the case from happening.

FRV chief fire officer Mick Tisbury has spent the past 15 years advocating for Australian firefighters and communities affected by exposure to PFAS via firefighting foam.

3M Company has manufactured PFAS chemicals since the 1950s. (Supplied: 3M)

PFAS is a class of synthetic chemicals used in many products, including firefighting foams created by 3M.

It has been linked to numerous health concerns such as birth defects, neurological effects, kidney failure, and a variety of cancers.

According to testimony given in the US House of Representatives in 2019, 3M discovered PFAS chemicals were toxic in its own animal studies as early as the 1950s, but it did not begin phasing them out until 2003.

Mr Tisbury told ABC Regional Victoria's Statewide Drive program he hoped the federal government's decision to sue 3M would be a "kick-starter" for the Victorian government to re-examine the class action that was blocked three years ago.

Mick Tisbury has spent 15 years fighting for firefighters affected by PFAS. (Australian Story: Scott Jewell)

"It's good to see this happening and the perpetrators hopefully will be held to account," Mr Tisbury said.

" [3M] knew for decades and decades and decades how harmful [PFAS] was, not only to the environment, but also for firefighters, and they made billions of dollars off … firefighters, who unfortunately I've got to attend their funerals. "

He said the Commonwealth's legal action would send "a powerful message, not only to 3M, but all those other multinational chemical companies that they've got to prove that something's safe rather than those victims have to prove something's unsafe".

Hopes for blocked case

Mr Tisbury said he believed the federal government's decision to sue 3M was vindication for the abandoned class action on behalf of Australian firefighters.

"The attorney general and our minister at the time Jaclyn Symes and we worked on it for three or four years with the Victorian government solicitors' office [and] crown counsel … [and] some heavy-hitting legal firms," he said.

"At the end of the day, we needed the minister to sign off on it and she didn't, for reasons I don't agree with.

"Hopefully, this decision by the feds has vindicated our position and who knows, maybe [our case] could be revisited now.

"We were going to be the lead litigant and every other fire service was going to jump on board, so hopefully that can be resurrected."

PFAS firefighting foam was used widely prior to 2000, including in training days. (Supplied: Fire Brigade Employees' Union)

The aim of the class action was to force 3M to pay for all past, present and future clean-ups undertaken as a result of the use of its products.

"It shouldn't be the taxpayer footing the bill for all the remediation and the work, it should be the perpetrator, 3M, who knew about it and covered it up," Mr Tisbury said.

"FRV is spending millions of dollars on remediating our fire stations where this legacy contamination still exists.

"We've spent millions of dollars on a world-first blood treatment to get this stuff out of our bodies.

"We've spent millions of bucks on developing the process to clean our fire trucks from the legacy contamination.

" There will be millions and millions and millions of dollars required to continue the work that we're doing, so 3M should be picking up the tab for that one. "

The toll

Mr Tisbury said he had been with FRV for 38 years, and had spent 15 of those years advocating for firefighters affected by PFAS.

"Every fight you have takes a bit more spark off you, so I'm a bit cooked," he said.

"It's a new attorney general now and we've got a new minister as well, so I'll have a conversation and see if there's an appetite [to restart the class action].

" Fifteen years of smacking your head against a wall, it takes its toll, but this decision by the feds does actually give me a bit of hope. "

When the ABC asked Ms Symes in November last year about preventing the class action from proceeding, she did not respond to specific questions about it.

Jaclyn Symes blocked a class action to be led by FRV against 3M when she was Victoria's emergency services minister. (AAP: Joel Carrett)

In a letter seen by the ABC dated August 2023, Ms Symes notified FRV she would not provide consent for it to enter into the class action litigation against 3M, citing "significant and complex legal and public policy risks" and "reputational risks" as the reasons for the withdrawal.

A state government spokesperson told the ABC on Thursday that it was "on the side of firefighters who put their health and lives on the line to keep Victorians safe".

"We welcome the federal government's announcement to launch legal action against 3M and are considering our position," the spokesperson said.

title: John Farnham says he won't ever sing on stage again ahead of tribute concert
text:
A tribute concert has been announced for Australian music legend John Farnham, with Celine Dion, Hugh Jackman and Keith Urban among the stars set to perform at the show later this year.

The concert, dubbed The Songs of John Farnham: A Living Legend, will be held at Melbourne's Rod Laver Arena on September 20, with funds raised on the night going toward the cancer service that supported Farnham during his cancer battle.

Farnham, 76, has told the Herald Sun he will not be singing on stage again, telling the publication it was "just not possible" for him to perform following cancer-related mouth and facial surgeries.

Jimmy Barnes, Tina Arena, Human Nature, Jessica Mauboy, Kate Ceberano, Ross Wilson and Richard Marx are some of the other big names touted to perform at the concert.

Dion and Jackman will be performing from overseas via satellite.

Keith Urban and Jessica Mauboy will perform at the September concert. (Getty Images: Paul Miller)

Farnham was diagnosed with mouth cancer in 2022 and he was hospitalised with a respiratory infection in March 2023.

In August 2023, Farnham confirmed he was cancer free.

His last public performance was as part of Sydney's Fire Fight fundraising concert in February 2020.

John Farnham has been one of Australia's most beloved music performers for decades. (Supplied: National Film and Sound Archives)

Funds raised at the concert will be donated to Head and Neck Cancer Australia.

In a statement Farnham said the night was about "giving something back and supporting a cause that means a great deal to me".

"The fact that so many extraordinary artists from around the world have so generously agreed to be part of this night is incredibly humbling," he said.

"[It] says far more about the spirit behind this event than it does about me."

Ahead of the concert, Dion said it was "an honour and a privilege for me to be included to celebrate the extraordinary legacy John has given to music".

"John Farnham is truly one of the great voices of the world," she said.

Celine Dion will perform at the concert from Paris via satellite. (AP: Jacques Boissinot/The Canadian Press)

"I have such beautiful memories of the time we shared on stage together — his generosity, his humour, and that incredible voice that fills your heart the moment you hear it."

Farnham is one of Australia's most successful performers, with his 1986 album Whispering Jack selling more than 1.6 million copies.

He was named Australian of the Year in 1987 and has won 21 ARIA awards.

In 2024 Farnham released his first memoir, The Voice Inside, which details the monumental triumphs of his career as well as the immense struggles he has faced.

Tickets for the concert go on sale on June 2.

title: Refugee who tried to flee the Taliban 15 times finally safe to play cricket
text:
It was love at first sight when Firooza Afghan watched her first cricket match.

Seeing the Afghan flag being raised before the game and the crack of the ball colliding with a cricket bat filled her with national pride.

In her hometown of Herat, she was determined to set up a women's league, starting with her own school cricket team.

It was years later she got the call — a women's Afghanistan cricket team had been established, and she had been selected to play.

But before they could take to the pitch, the Taliban took over and Firooza's hometown was the first major city to fall.

Firooza Afghan has always been passionate about cricket. (ABC News: Marcus Kennedy)

Suddenly her life wasn't centred around cricket: her focus was on survival.

With the Taliban returned to power in Afghanistan, women's sport was effectively banned.

Firooza destroyed most of her cricket gear to stay safe, but couldn't bear to burn her Afghanistan cricket shirt, which meant so much to her.

One of her teammates had an Australian mentor and, with their help, Firooza and her family planned to leave — though it took 15 attempts before they made a successful escape.

Firooza Afghan and her family are now refugees in Australia. (ABC News: Marcus Kennedy)

"My journey was a bit different because my family didn't have a passport," she said.

"We went to Pakistan illegally.

" We left our country, not because we wanted to — we left to keep our dream up. "

After nine months in Pakistan, her family was able to seek refuge in Australia.

Now, most of her cricket squad is based here too.

"Finding days in summer and playing cricket, it's pretty much a happy ending for us," Firooza said.

Many women had to leave Afghanistan in order to pursue cricket. (ABC News: Marcus Kennedy)

An 'incredibly challenging' journey

Emma Staples, who co-founded initiatives Pitch Our Future and It's Game On to champion gender equality in Australian sports, has been with the team since they fled Afghanistan.

"The journey for all of them was incredibly challenging," she said.

"Their evacuation was by road, so we had to get everyone into Kabul.

"I can't imagine what that's like, the terror of crossing your country in such a volatile time."

Emma Staples has been a mentor for the Afghan cricketers, helping them adjust to life in Australia. (ABC News: Marcus Kennedy)

After helping the athletes settle in Australia, she has formed strong bonds with the women and their families.

"I couldn't be prouder of these women," Emma said.

" This group of women are still fighting and advocating so strongly for their recognition in international cricket. "

The athletes are are still fighting for recognition. (ABC News: Marcus Kennedy)

Earlier this year, FIFA recognised displaced Afghan players as eligible for international competition, clearing the way for women footballers to return to their passion.

Firooza is calling on the International Cricket Council (ICC) to follow suit.

"We are getting a chance to represent our team at international levels, hopefully my team can play in the world cup as well," the cricketer said.

" This team have talent [and] have the skills, and they have the same dreams as the Afghanistan men's team. "

In a letter penned to the ICC and made public in March, Human Rights Watch suggested the "Taliban-run Afghanistan's membership" should be immediately suspended "until women and girls can participate in the sport of cricket".

The ABC has contacted the ICC for comment.

From the NT to the UK

Years on from her escape from Afghanistan, Firooza has been touring Darwin as part of the Afghan Refugee Women's XI team.

The cricket team will soon tour the UK. (ABC News: Marcus Kennedy)

This month's tournament included one 40-over and two T20 matches against the Strike — the first women's team to represent the Northern Territory in cricket since 2019.

The tour comes in the lead-up to the Afghan Refugee Women's XI tour next month, when they will travel to the United Kingdom to compete.

title: 'Everyone thought Sinner would win': French Open upset stuns tennis world
text:
Not since Rafael Nadal was winning his record 14 French Opens had a player come to Roland-Garros as such an overwhelming favourite to win the clay-court major.

Jannik Sinner had won everything there was to win in tennis over the past three months: five straight Masters 1000 titles — three of them on clay — and 30 consecutive matches.

Sinner exits French Open Jannik Sinner's French Open campaign is over after the world number one struggles with illness in a shock second-round defeat to Juan Manuel Cerúndolo.

And with Carlos Alcaraz, his biggest rival, ruled out due to an injured right wrist, it seemed almost a foregone conclusion Sinner would raise the Coupe des Mousquetaires trophy and complete a career Grand Slam.

That's why Sinner's meltdown amid the Paris heat wave was so stunning, especially after he came within just one game of concluding his second-round match in straight sets when he led 5-1 in the third.

The top-ranked Sinner struggled with dizziness and was beaten by 56th-ranked Argentine Juan Manuel Cerúndolo 3-6, 2-6, 7-5, 6-1, 6-1 after wasting two chances to serve for the match.

"I didn't feel very well on court," Sinner said.

"I struggled, starting to feel very dizzy, very low on energy … in the beginning, I was hitting very clean, very good, and then I just kind of hit the wall."

Juan Manuel Cerúndolo (left) stunned the tennis world with his win over Jannik Sinner. (Getty Images: Marleen Fouchier)

Sinner's previous loss came on February 19 in the Qatar Open quarterfinals.

He had won five straight Masters titles while dropping just three sets.

"We'll definitely do some [medical] tests to be sure of what happened today," Sinner said.

"Let's hope we're ready for Wimbledon. To be ready there, we need to recuperate well and do things right now."

But Sinner has a history of struggling in the heat.

Sinner 'got lucky' Had the Australian Open's extreme heat protocols not taken effect, Janik Sinner wonders if he would still be in the tournament.

He admitted he "got lucky" at the Australian Open in January against Eliot Spizzirri when the roof was closed and the third-round match swung his way.

And he had to retire from a match in Shanghai in October that was contested amid extreme humidity.

"Shanghai was very tough. Humidity very high. Australia was very, very warm," Sinner said.

"Here, I mean it was warm, but it was OK. It was not like I was dying because of the heat. I think today was completely different scenario.

"It's tough to accept, of course, because of the position where I've been in and everything considered."

'Lot of things can happen'

Sinner's exit and Alcaraz's absence have left the men's draw wide open, with players sensing a rare chance to claim a major title.

The "Sincaraz" duo, still in their early 20s, have won the last nine majors, with 39-year-old Novak Djokovic still looking like the player who most likely to disrupt their dominance.

While the Serbian will be the favourite to capture a record-extending 25th major trophy in Paris, American Learner Tien said more ‌challengers were likely to have renewed belief.

"I can't speak for anyone else, honestly, but from what I feel all the young guys coming up want to prove themselves," the ‌20-year-old Tien told reporters.

"I think a lot of the guys coming up really have a lot of belief in ⁠themselves, that they belong at this level and that they can challenge the ‌top guys for these events.

"I think that's huge. Just believing is a huge first ‌step and a lot of these younger guys, myself included, are just very eager to go out and prove themselves."

Jannik Sinner did all he could to keep his cool. (Getty Images: Mustafa Yalcin)

Canadian Félix Auger-Aliassime said Cerundolo's win had put him firmly in the spotlight.

"Everyone thought Sinner would win this year's French Open," Auger-Aliassime said.

"It's not going to be him. I guess that's the beauty of sports. Not so ‌great for him. But at the same time, everyone will remember what happened with Juan Manuel.

"He's a great player with many qualities and it's going to shed great light on him. Sinner is fine. He's got ⁠all the light that he needs and he'll be grand."

American ‌Frances Tiafoe said the rest of ‌the field had more opportunities now Sinner had gone.

"It would all boil down to how players react to ‌the opponent in front of them on any given day," he said.

"Now that he's out, I think everyone is going to have more but ultimately it doesn't really matter. You've got to beat ‌one guy and whoever is in front ⁠of you.

"It's a long two weeks. A lot of things can happen."

Reuters

title: Last working steam tram of its kind gets two new drivers
text:
Very few people in the world can say they have driven a Purrey steam tram, and as of this week that number has increased by two.

"It's very, very cool, it's quite a thrill," new driver Jo Webb said.

" It's the last working one in the world. We're very lucky to have it and we're very lucky she's still going. "

The crowd at the opening of the Rockhampton Tramways on June 5, 1909. (State Library of Queensland)

Steam trams are a rarity across the globe, and the only Purrey-type engine still running sits inside a museum in the central Queensland town of Rockhampton.

Emma Barnes at the Rockhampton Archer Park Rail Museum. (ABC Capricornia: Vanessa Jarrett)

Emma Barnes is the coordinator of rail safety at the Archer Park Rail Museum.

"We have heard that there is one in France and it is on display but non-operational," she said.

Volunteers ready to hit the rail

Michael Feldman-Budarick and Jo Webb behind the handles of the Purrey steam tram in Rockhampton. (ABC Capricornia: Vanessa Jarrett)

Museum volunteers Michael Feldman-Budarick and Ms Webb have being working hard to earn a certificate in advanced boilermaking which allows them to drive the historic tram.

"After a long, strenuous test and a lot to learn, I'm so proud of myself for achieving it," Mr Feldman-Budarick said.

The Purrey steam tram pictured in Rockhampton in 1932. (State Library of Queensland)

The Purrey system, designed by French civil engineer Valentin Purrey, was popular across Europe in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

The Rockhampton fleet, which included nine tramcars and six trailers, ran from 1909 to 1939, carried more than 40 million passengers and collected more than £350,000 in fares.

That would be close to $38 million today.

"We owe a lot to the Purrey trams making transportation happen in Rocky," Mr Feldman-Budarick said.

This postcard from 1914 depicts the Rockhampton central business district. (State Library of Queensland)

Back from the terminus

The growing use of buses and private motor cars in the late 1930s meant the trams began to lose money for the council, which was also dealing with deteriorating tracks and parts.

Decades later a remnant of the original tram was found, inspiring the council to launch a restoration project.

The tram was restored using parts gathered from relics from all over. (ABC Capricornia: Vanessa Jarrett)

The rebuilt tram was officially recommissioned on June 5, 1988.

It was moved to the Archer Park Rail Museum in 1999 where it is looked after by volunteers and taken out for rides on Sundays and during the school holidays.

A digitised image of Rockhampton in 1923. The Purrey steam trams ran in the city from 1909 to 1939. (State Library of Queensland)

"This tram is a bit peculiar. It was designed in France, so it's actually got a slightly different arrangement to any train you might see in a heritage railway in Australia," Ms Webb said.

"There's all sorts of gadgets in there, there's valves to open, valves to close, you've got to make sure you're going the right way.

"There's a gear stick but fortunately you don't have a clutch."

Jo Webb driving the only working Purrey steam tram in the world, up the track in Rockhampton. (ABC Capricornia: Vanessa Jarrett)

Ms Webb is the first female to stand behind the handles of the tram in the museum's history.

"They've had female conductors before, but they've never had anyone advance to driver status," she said.

"I'm actually a bit chuffed."

Protecting Purrey for the future

Mr Feldman-Budarick has been involved with the museum for the past decade, moving through the ranks from tour guide to conductor.

"I've always been fascinated by trains since I was three years old. Started out with Thomas and Friends and grew up to the bigger trains," he said.

There were nine tramcars and six trailers in the fleet at Rockhampton, built in Bordeaux, France. This photograph was taken circa 1920. (State Library of Queensland: J Kenneth Brown)

Being 28 years old he is a younger volunteer among some more mature faces.

"You learn experience from other people who have spent years doing this, like the drivers who have taught me. They've spent a lot of years in the railway as well," he said.

"They can teach you things that you can take on to the next, into many generations."

title: This comedian has 1.7m followers. He doesn't think it's real
text:
When Will Gibb answers my call, he's driving around Brisbane singing along to Charli XCX's "Wuthering Heights" hit Chains of Love. Loudly.

It takes a beat for the comedian, TikToker and actor to acknowledge someone's on the other end of the line.

In his defence, it's a busy time.

Firstly, it's comedy festival season, with the standup in the sunshine state for a run of his latest live show, following performances in Sydney and Melbourne.

But he's also managing posting to his two million-strong audience online and doing press for his first major acting gig.

Which brings us to the reason for this interview: we want Gibb to tell us everything about starring as Kitty Flanagan's son Ryan in new sitcom Bad Company, created by fellow comic Anne Edmonds.

The result is an occasionally off-colour chat that covers everything from his journey to standup, to acting, to the reality of being online-famous.

Stream Bad Company now on ABC iview Margie, the unstable artistic director of the iconic theatre company, The Argyle, is up for the fight of her life, when Julia is hi-jacked from her corporate career to save the place from financial ruin.

How did you get into comedy?

In Year 7, I was doing improv and one of the teachers was like, "Hey, you're really funny, come and do improv with the older boys."

And then I was like 12 doing improv with the 17-year-olds, and I felt really cool.

And then how did that lead to standup?

When I was 16, I heard about [the Class Clowns] comedy competition and I signed up for it and yeah, I won the NSW state final so hard. They said no-one had ever won it this hard before, and I was the best comedian they'd ever seen.

Women were throwing themselves at me, I had to say, "I'm gay, I'm gay, stop."

It was a whole thing, you can look it up.

In his first major role in a national television show, Gibb plays Kitty Flanagan's "sad son" Ryan. (ABC/Guesswork Television: Sarah Enticknap)

OK, I'm sure there are countless articles about that online. What happened next?

Then I bombed my absolute tits off at the Melbourne Town Hall in the Class Clowns final, but I still stuck with comedy.

And it paid off, clearly! What's it like having 1.7 million followers on TikTok?

Uh, it's great. It means I'm better than everyone else. Anyone who has less followers than me is a loser, and I don't have any interest in them whatsoever.

And on a serious note?

Kind of nothing, honestly. It's so whatever, it's fake, it doesn't really exist. I actually do not think it's real.

What's the hardest thing about translating online success into real-world opportunities?

I think people maybe have a bit of a preconceived idea of what TikTokers are capable of.

Vertical content is what most of the world is consuming and we're just the best at being able to create that. And I believe if there was another form of content that was at its height, we'd be able to do the same.

I think we're boxed into only being able to do this thing, but in actual reality we recognise what people like. We recognise how they like it to be filmed and shot and edited and we're able to present that to people. And I think we're able to do that not just in this medium, but also on stage and through TV and film.

Do you come up against that sentiment a lot?

I feel really lucky, because I've been doing standup so long that I'm starting to feel like I'm legitimate, but it's very [ingrained]. I shouldn't feel the need to have to be constantly trying to legitimise myself, yet I do.

The project that inspired Anne Edmonds' satire of the arts world Everything Anne Edmonds has done over the last two decades has been leading to Bad Company.

But that's fine because it pushes me to actually go and do that stuff, so yeah.

Acting in a national TV show is indeed 'doing the stuff'. What made you want to play Ryan, specifically?

The fact Eddo asked me was an honour, an absolute honour.

And then I would say money … and fame.

In that order?

Yes, Eddo, money, fame. Put it on a shirt and sell it, that's merch right there.

Also, I related to feeling a bit lost and not knowing what to do with my life, which is Ryan's character arc.

How much influence were you able to have on him as a character?

Loading Instagram content

Eddo gave me so much freedom and so did [series director] Tom Peterson to play with lines. I had so much freedom, which was really cool.

What elements of yourself did you lend Ryan?

Raw sex appeal. [Laughs] Uh, no … Maybe just the eagerness to impress and please people. But also, a stubbornness.

Did Ryan's 'sad son' quality come from you?

Kitty made that up when she looked at me for the first time.

I think I have the quality of the saddest son.

Really?

No, that was already in the script.

What was it like starring alongside not one, but two Australian comedy greats in Bad Company?

I kind of didn't really process it. I've loved Anne forever, she's a massive inspiration. She used to do this bit about this drunk girl falling down the stairs and she used to yell out, "It's not OK!"

Me and my mum used to yell that at each other all the time.

Anne Edmonds' character sees Ryan's gullible nature as the key to bringing down his mother, who's her workplace rival. (ABC/Guesswork Television: Sarah Enticknap)

And Kitty's just obviously an icon as well, who I learned about more recently, and I love everything she does.

They taught me how to yell at runners and be really rude and yell at the crew …

No, I'm joking. Eddo and Kitty are both such lovely people outside of the show.

You've just been at the comedy festivals in Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane. What's next?

Next is a sleep. It's like, "Far out, this has been a lot". I might do Perth later, though.

Quotes lightly edited for clarity and brevity.

title: Bleijie backs private school’s move to clear bushland as koala spotted
text:
Advertisement

Updated NationalQueenslandEnvironmental protection Bleijie backs private school’s move to clear bushland as koala spotted Cameron Atfield Updated May 29, 2026 — 1:51pm ,first published May 28, 2026 — 8:01pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Queensland’s deputy premier has defended his decision to allow a bayside private school to clear bushland for new sport fields, a day after GPS tracked another koala entering the area, which was already being cleared. Protests have been held outside Ormiston College since Deputy Premier Jarrod Bleijie green-lit the destruction of contested koala habitat to make way for the expansion of the private school’s sporting fields last week. “Leave koalas alone” has been graffitied on the college’s welcome sign, and at least one person, former Greens candidate Kristie Lockhart, has been arrested for jumping a fence. Graffiti outside Ormiston College. Facebook In a statement to this masthead on Friday, after publication of this story, college principal Michael Hornby said the school respected the community’s right to protest.

Advertisement

GPS tracking has shown Leaf in the Ormiston College bushland being cleared. Redlands Koala Action Group The graffiti, however, had caused some concern. “The safety of our students and staff is always our first priority and we are conscious that this escalation has caused some concern, however, we are in direct and regular contact with our school community so they are kept informed and supported throughout this entire process,” Hornby said. Hornby said the construction area was identified through “multiple ecological surveys conducted between 2016 and 2023” as a koala transit corridor. “[It is] not a breeding or high-density habitat,” he said.

Advertisement

“While koalas have been sighted on the grounds over recent years, these are koalas in transit, not permanent residents.” Redland City Council has disputed the ecological report on which Hornby relied to reach that conclusion – although it said it supported the project in principle. On Thursday, the Redlands Koala Action Group reported a GPS-tracked koala named Leaf had moved on to the Ormiston College site overnight. Leaf survived a car strike that left her blind in one eye and separated from her missing joey last year, and was released into bushland after months of rehabilitation. “This loss of this significant mapped core koala habitat is absolutely devastating to Leaf and the other koalas that call this area home and makes a mockery of the state government’s new koala strategy,” Redlands Koala Action Group president Debbie Pointing said.

Advertisement

“It’s been heartbreaking for the community to witness century-old trees that we know are regularly utilised by Leaf and other koalas simply being cut down for sporting ovals is morally wrong.” Speaking at Howard Smith Wharves on Thursday afternoon, Bleijie defended his decision and said it was based on independent advice from the State Development and Planning Department. Related Article Brisbane development Private school given go-ahead to cut down koala habitat for new ovals “You could complain about every MID [ministerial infrastructure designation] that every former Labor government had done,” he said. “The planning ministers do the infrastructure designations all the time – it’s nothing new, and that is simply what we’ve done in this case.

Advertisement

“And I’ve seen the comments from the principal – I think they were very good comments – and I’ve also seen again protesters illegally trespassing, attacking a college, a school in that area, and graffitiing the outside of it. “We’ll treat everyone respectfully, but people have to abide by the laws and Ormiston College is applying and abiding by the laws in place.” Protesters outside the college this week. Queensland Koala Conservation Collective - Facebook Redland City Council denied a similar application from Ormiston College to add an oval and a slew of buildings around the campus in 2021, but the school took new plans directly to the state government last April. A petition to Bleijie urging him to reject the plan, which would remove 652 mature trees, attracted 8300 signatures.

Advertisement

Pointing said Leaf’s movements were a clear demonstration of how important the site was to the local koala population. “Then to hear yesterday the college principal say it’s not habitat, and any koalas on the site are lost, is sheer ignorance and denial,” she said. Pointing said the Koala Action Group had asked the federal government to call in the project and overrule Bleijie’s decision. A spokesperson for the Federal Environment Department said while they were aware of the Ormiston College project, they had not received a referral. They added that it was the school’s responsibility to refer the proposal for assessment, “if a significant impact on matters of national environmental significance is likely”.

Advertisement

“In this instance, the proponent is aware of their obligations under national environmental law, and a self-assessment was undertaken and included in the Queensland government Ministerial Infrastructure Designation approval documents,” the spokesperson said.
